title: Beef and Guinness stew
subtitle: Slow-braised beef in dark stout with root vegetables
excerpt: Braising steak cooked very slowly in Guinness and beef stock with onions, carrots, and a little thyme until the sauce is dark, glossy, and the beef pulls apart. One of the most satisfying cold-weather braises.
sourceNotes: Beef braised in Guinness or Irish stout is a well-documented tradition in Irish and British pub cooking, widely recorded from the second half of the twentieth century. The combination of stout and beef appeared in popular British cookbooks from the 1970s onwards.

content.text: The bitterness of Guinness rounds out in a long braise, leaving a dark, slightly sweet undertone in the sauce. It should not taste of beer at the end; it should taste of slow-cooked beef with a depth that water or wine alone cannot provide. The key is long, gentle heat: two and a half to three hours at a bare simmer, not a rolling boil.

Braising steak or chuck gives the best result: the fat and collagen in these cuts dissolve into the sauce over time, producing a glossy braise. Lean stewing steak will produce a thin sauce and dry meat. Do not rush the browning at the start: the deep colour on the meat and vegetables is the foundation of the sauce.

Dust {{braising-steak}} of beef pieces in seasoned flour. Heat a generous layer of oil in a Dutch oven over high heat and brown the beef in batches until deep brown on all sides. Do not rush this. Set the beef aside. Reduce the heat to medium and cook {{onion}} roughly chopped onion and {{carrot}} carrot pieces for 8 minutes until beginning to colour at the edges. Add {{garlic}} of crushed garlic and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and cook for 1 minute.

Pour in {{guinness}} of Guinness and let it bubble for 2 minutes, scraping the base. Add {{stock-beef}} of beef stock and {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce. Return the beef to the pot with the thyme and bay leaves. Bring to a gentle simmer, cover tightly, and cook over the lowest possible heat for 2 to 2.5 hours.

After 2 hours the beef should be very tender and the sauce dark and glossy. Remove the lid and cook for a further 20 minutes to reduce the sauce if it is thin. Remove the thyme and bay leaves. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Scatter with chopped parsley if using. Serve with mashed potato, colcannon, or buttered noodles.

items.fix: Lower the heat further and continue cooking. Beef braised at too high a temperature seizes rather than relaxes. An extra 30 to 45 minutes at a bare simmer will usually rescue it.
items.cause: Heat was too high; the braise boiled rather than simmered

items.fix: Continue cooking uncovered. A pinch of brown sugar will soften bitterness if it persists. The flavour should mellow with more time.
items.cause: The Guinness bitterness was not fully cooked out
items.symptom: Sauce has a sharp, bitter taste

items.fix: Remove the lid and reduce at a gentle simmer for 20 to 30 minutes. Alternatively, remove a ladleful of sauce, whisk in a teaspoon of cornflour, and return to the pot.
items.cause: Too much liquid or the flour did not thicken adequately

items.fix: Add the carrots for the last 45 minutes only if you prefer them to hold shape. In the traditional version, soft vegetables that meld into the sauce are not considered a problem.
items.cause: Cooked for too long at too high a temperature
items.symptom: Carrots have dissolved into the sauce

content.text: Beef braised in Guinness is a pub dish from Ireland and Britain. It is a pot made to be kept warm and served over several days. It gets better with each reheat. The dish is not a hard one. The quality of the braise depends almost entirely on patience: low heat, long time, and not lifting the lid.
